coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance UV photoresponsivity and surface-related sensing speed of zinc oxide nanorod thin films
coredata.dc:creator: Rahimi, Kourosh
Yazdani, Ahmad
Ahmadirad, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Thin films of almost vertically-grown zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NR) were prepared via a solvothermal method, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid. Various amounts of GQDs were loaded onto ZnO NR thin films by applying a dip-coating process for different times. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray diffractometry (XRD) were employed to characterize the structures. UV–visible spectra of the thin films showed no significant change in the bandgap (~3.3eV) after incorporating GQDs, which is appropriate for visible-blind UV sensing applications. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra showed a significant decrease in the PL intensity of the ZnO NR thin film after incorporating GQDs, suggesting that the generated electrons can be better separated from their hole counterparts and hence contribute to the generated photocurrent. Finally, UV sensing analysis was performed, indicating that GQDs can enhance both the UV-induced photocurrent and the sensing speed of ZnO NR thin films; however, the improvement in the surface-related UV sensing was higher than in the bulk-related one, which could be attributed to the numerous active sites and edge effects of GQDs. The enhanced photocurrent was discussed based on the band diagram.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide nanorod
Thin films
Graphene quantum dot
UV sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of thin films of ZnO nanorods
Fabrication of ZnO NR/GQD thin films
Mechanism of UV sensing in ZnO
Mechanism of UV sensing enhancement by GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Left): UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs suspension and a photograph of the suspension in a quartz cuvette. Tauc plot of the absorption curve is also shown. (Right): Photoluminescence spectrum of the GQDs suspension excited by 365nm. A photograph of the excited GQDs suspension is also shown.
Raman spectrum of the prepared GQDs.
(a) An AFM micrograph (1μm∗1μm) of GQDs on a mica surface and the height profile of the shown line. A TEM image the prepared GQDs is also shown in the inset. (b) A FESEM image of ZnO NRs.
XRD patterns of the bare ZnO NR and 4GQD-ZnO NR thin films.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the bare ZnO NR thin film and the nGQD-ZnO NR thin films (n =1 to 4, indicates the number of applied dip-coating procedures). Tauc plots of the spectra are also shown in the inset.
PL spectra of the bare ZnO NR thin film and 4GQD-ZnO NR thin film.
Passing currents versus time of the bare ZnO and nGQD-ZnO NR thin films under UV irradiation.
Recorded and fitted UV sensing transient data of the bare ZnO NR and the 3GQD-ZnO NR thin films.
Energy band diagram of the GQD-ZnO NR composite and its carrier transport mechanism in the interfacial region when excited by UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots enhance UV photoresponsivity and surface-related sensing speed of zinc oxide nanorod thin films

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Condensed Matter Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thin films of almost vertically-grown zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NR) were prepared via a solvothermal method, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid. Various amounts of GQDs were loaded onto ZnO NR thin films by applying a dip-coating process for different times. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray diffractometry (XRD) were employed to characterize the structures. UV–visible spectra of the thin films showed no significant change in the bandgap (~3.3eV) after incorporating GQDs, which is appropriate for visible-blind UV sensing applications. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra showed a significant decrease in the PL intensity of the ZnO NR thin film after incorporating GQDs, suggesting that the generated electrons can be better separated from their hole counterparts and hence contribute to the generated photocurrent. Finally, UV sensing analysis was performed, indicating that GQDs can enhance both the UV-induced photocurrent and the sensing speed of ZnO NR thin films; however, the improvement in the surface-related UV sensing was higher than in the bulk-related one, which could be attributed to the numerous active sites and edge effects of GQDs. The enhanced photocurrent was discussed based on the band diagram.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet (UV) light sensors are employed in various applications such as in detection of missile plume, environmental monitoring, medical equipment, communications, imaging, etc. [1–4]. Modern UV sensors are usually made of wide-bandgap semiconductors, like ZnO, which has a wide and direct bandgap of ~3.37eV (in its bulk state) at room temperature and also a very large excitonic binding energy (60meV), which is suitable for devices operating at room temperature [5,6]. ZnO has been widely investigated for applications such as photocatalysts, LEDs, solar cells, photodetectors, and chemical sensors [7–18]. One of the novel and currently under investigation applications of ZnO is in the field of ultraviolet light sensing [19–21]. On the one hand, it does not require any sophisticated filter to filter out visible light (in contrast to silicon-based UV sensors) and hence it is almost visible-blind by its nature. On the other hand, it is sensitive to nearly entire UV region of light (in contrast to extremely wide-bandgap semiconductors like aluminum nitride with a bandgap as wide as 6eV).

As the size of ZnO (like any other material) scales down to the nanometer region, its surface area/volume ratio increases, and hence many more active sites are produced on surfaces that can participate in reactions with light or other chemicals. Various shapes of ZnO nanostructures have been prepared and examined so far for UV sensing applications [22–24]. One-dimensional (1D) ZnO nanostructures, such as nanorods, nanowires, and nanotubes, have two major advantages: (a) their high surface/volume ratio, leading to deep surface traps that are suitable for collecting charge carriers and increasing their lifetime, and (b) their easier longitudinal charge carrier transportation due to their rod shape that can contribute to the generated photocurrent [25]. Designing ZnO nanorods as nearly aligned on a substrate is an attempt to have a high surface area to volume ratio and while utilizing the charge transportation made possible due to the rod-like shape.

However, there is a major challenge that should be solved before the widespread use of ZnO as UV sensors could be realized, which is the extremely fast recombination of its photogenerated e–h pairs. An approach to resolving the problem is by modifying ZnO surface to create trapping sites, which prevent electrons and holes from recombination. Another approach is by combining ZnO with other elements or structures, for example, graphene [26–28].

Graphene sheets with their very large specific surface and very high electron mobility [29,30] have been combined with ZnO nanostructures to enhance their UV responsivity by separating the photogenerated electrons from their hole counterparts and providing a large surface for transferring the electrons. Recently, graphene quantum dots have also been used to enhance UV sensing properties of ZnO [31–36]. Graphene quantum dots are single- or few-layer graphene sheets with a very small size usually under 100nm. In addition to the unique properties inherited from graphene sheets, GQDs also have some advantages over their mother structure for UV sensing applications: (a) their higher penetrability into the pores and small spaces because of their remarkably smaller size, (b) their semiconducting nature [37] that gives them a lower conductivity, because a high conductivity can make ZnO thin films conductive even under a small bias, making ZnO ineffective for generating signal upon excitation by UV light, and (c) their many more active edges. Moreover, if we can design ZnO nanorods as nearly aligned, much space can be provided to accommodate graphene quantum dots.

Here, in order to utilize the advantages of GQDs mentioned above for enhancing UV sensing properties of ZnO and also to study the effect of GQDs amount on UV-induced photoresponsivity of ZnO NR thin film and to achieve the optimum case, we loaded various amounts of GQDs onto the ZnO NR thin films by applying a dip coating process for different times. Finally, we discussed the mechanism behind the UV photoresponsivity enhancement of ZnO NR thin films based on the band diagram of the composite. By fitting a double-process exponential rise and decay equation on the transient UV sensing data, we attributed the observed enhancement to the surface-related mechanism provided by GQDs.
All precursors were of analytical grade provided by Merck Co. Water used throughout the work was deionized water. All glass substrates were cleaned with acetone, ethanol and DI water.

Pyrolysis of citric acid [38] was performed to synthesize graphene quantum dots. In a typical experiment, a glass tube containing 2g citric acid in an oil bath was heated by a heater at 200°C for 15min. The tube was then let to naturally cool to room temperature, and an aqueous NaOH solution (10mg/mL) was added to it while vigorously being stirred to increase its pH value to 7. The prepared suspension was kept in a refrigerator to prevent the GQDs from aggregation. The obtained GQDs suspension was used without further dilution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of thin films of ZnO nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO NR thin films were prepared in two main steps: (1) synthesizing ZnO nanoparticles as seeds on glass substrates by a sol-gel method and (2) growing ZnO nanorods on the pre-seeded substrates in a chemical bath by a solvothermal growth method. In the first step, a solution containing 0.05M zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) and 0.06M diethanolamine (HN(CH2CH2OH)2) was prepared, and it was stirred for 30min at 60°C to become clear. The prepared solution was spin-coated (2000rpm for 30s) on glass substrates with a size of 2cm∗2cm, which were subsequently dried at 200°C for 10min to evaporate the residual solvent and organic materials. The spin-coating procedure was repeated for totally four times to achieve a sufficient and uniform thickness. Finally, the substrates were annealed at 500°C for 1h in a muffle furnace to crystallize the ZnO nanoparticles on the substrates to be used as pre-seeded substrates. In the second step, an aqueous solution containing 0.001M zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and 0.1M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was prepared at 70°C, and the pre-seeded substrates were suspended upside down into the solution so that the ZnO nanorods could grow vertically on the substrates. The substrates were then rinsed with DI water and subsequently annealed at 500°C for 1h to promote the ZnO crystallization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZnO NR/GQD thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The dip-coating method was employed to coat the synthesized GQDs on the prepared ZnO NR thin films. In a typical procedure, the GQDs suspension was poured into a 50mL beaker, with a height of 3cm, so that the films can be completely surrounded by the suspension. The prepared substrates were held vertically in the prepared GQDs suspension for 30s and pulled out with the speed of 30mm/min, and it was dried at 40°C in a vacuum furnace for 1h. The dip-coating procedure was repeated for different times (1 through 4). Finally, the substrates were annealed at 200°C for 1h to promote the formation of bonds between ZnO NRs and GQDs. The samples were named as nGQD-ZnO, where n (1 to 4) indicates the number of applied dip-coating procedures.

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X'Pert MPD, Philips) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ=1.5418Å) was employed to analyze the crystallographic structure of ZnO nanorods. A field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4160) and an atomic force microscope (AFM, Veeco Autoprobe CP-research) in contact mode with a Si tip of 10nm curvature radius in the air were employed to observe the samples' structures. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, CM30) was employed to observe the GQDs. Room-temperature photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were taken by a PL spectroscopy instrument (Gilden pλotonics equipped with a xenon lamp). The Raman spectrum of graphene quantum dots was taken by an Almega Thermo Nicolet Raman Spectrometer equipped with a Nd-YAG excitation laser with the wavelength of 532nm at room temperature. UV–vis spectra of the samples were recorded by a double-beam spectrophotometer (Unico, 4802). Bandgaps were calculated based on the Tauc relation, hν=A(hν−Eg)n, where h is the Plank constant, ν is the frequency of incident light, A is the absorption, Eg is the bandgap and n is a constant that depends on the nature (direct or indirect) of the bandgap. The intercept of a straight line along the linear part of the Tauc plot ((αhν)1/n versus hν) with the hν axis gives the bandgap.

To obtain an MSM structure, two simple gold electrodes with a distance of 1mm and length of 10mm were deposited on the thin films using an RF sputtering system over a precise shadow mask, so the active (uncoated) surface area of the thin films was 10mm2. A 5V bias was applied to the circuit, and UV photoresponsivity measurements were done in ambient air using an 8W Philips UVA dark light. A shutter was designed to allow and block the irradiation of UV source on the thin film instead of turning on and off the lamp, because the UV lamp needs time to warm up. The UV illumination intensity at the surface of the sensing element was 2mW/cm2.

Formation of GQDs was studied by UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry and photoluminescence spectrometry based on their characteristic peaks and PL emission. Fig. 1 shows the photoluminescence spectrum of the GQDs suspension excited by 365nm. It is seen that the GQDs suspension emits a spectrum peaked at 472nm (corresponding to the greenish blue color observed in the photograph, when it is excited by a UVA dark lamp). Fig. 1 also shows the UV–vis spectrum of the GQDs suspension in the wavelength range of 190–700nm. No considerable absorption is seen in the visible region. However, a strong absorption is observed in the ultraviolet region: a peak is seen at 240nm that corresponds to the π⟶π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 clusters, and a shoulder is seen in the range 320–350nm that corresponds to the n⟶π∗ transition of CO bonds [38–40]. The bandgap energy of the GQDs suspension was calculated as ~3.3eV from the Tauc plot ((αhν)2 versus hν for direct bandgap materials) of its UV–vis absorption spectrum, which is also shown in the inset of Fig. 1. This large bandgap is an evidence of the formation of GQDs, because graphene has a zero bandgap in theory [41] and when it becomes small enough that quantum confinement effects would dominate, it gains a bandgap [37].

Fig. 2 shows the Raman spectrum of the prepared GQDs. Both G and D bands, which are the characteristic bands of carbonaceous materials, can be seen, which are attributed to the sp2 graphitized structure and local defects/disorders, respectively [42,43]. The high ratio of D/G intensities indicates the numerous defects and disorders existing in the structure due to the GQD edges. Moreover, both bands are broad, which suggests the small sizes of GQDs.

AFM micrograph was taken to study the thickness and lateral size of the synthesized GQDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows an AFM micrograph and a thickness profile of the synthesized GQDs dropped on a mica surface. The AFM micrograph indicates that the GQDs are mostly nanosheets of 1–1.5nm in thickness (under 3 layers) and 10–40nm in diameter. A TEM image of the GQDs is also shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a), showing that the GQDs are of ~20nm diameter. The FESEM image was taken to observe the morphology of the ZnO NRs, as shown in Fig. 3(b). As can be seen, the nanorods are almost vertically grown, with an approximate diameter of ~70nm.

X-ray diffractometry was employed to characterize the crystal structure of the samples. Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of the bare ZnO NR and 4GQD-ZnO NR thin films. All diffraction peaks of the bare ZnO NR thin film, which are indicated in the figure, were matched with the JCPDS Card No. 01-079-0208, indicating that the obtained phase is hexagonal wurtzite. The main peak (002), at 2θ=34.3°, is sharp in both samples indicating the high crystallinity of the ZnO NRs that have preferentially grown along the c-axis of the wurtzite crystals, which is another evidence of the fact that the obtained ZnO structures have a nanorod shape. No other peak is observed in the pattern, indicating that there is no secondary phase and thus the sample does not contain any secondary phase or significant impurity. The ZnO crystallite size was estimated by the Scherrer equation, D=Kλ/βcos(θ), where λ is the incident radiation wavelength (here λ=1.5418Å), θ is the Bragg diffraction angle, and β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the Bragg diffraction. According to the equation, the ZnO crystallite size was calculated as 83nm, which is very close to the diameter of the ZnO NRs observed in the FESEM image (~70nm). The XRD pattern of the 4GQD-ZnO thin film is similar to that of the bare ZnO thin film, except for a broad band from 2θ=15° to 2θ=30°, which could be attributed to the presence of GQDs in the thin film. It has been shown in [38] that GQDs synthesized via the pyrolysis method show a wide band (002) around 0.34nm, due to their graphite structure.

To study the effect of loading GQDs on bandgaps of the ZnO NR thin films, UV–vis spectra of the prepared composite thin films were compared. Fig. 5 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the prepared thin films. As it is seen, absorption levels of the nGQD-ZnO thin films are higher than that of the bare ZnO thin film. Moreover, the absorption levels are monotonically increasing as n (the number of applied dip-coating procedures) increases; however, it seems that the absorption approaches a limiting value. It could be said that after the GQDs fully occupied the existing space and pores between the ZnO NRs, there is no more space for additional GQDs; hence, further dip-coating procedures lead to a very little GQDs loading, so they cannot significantly affect the absorption. Besides, the addition of GQDs does not alter the absorption of the thin films in the visible region, which is suitable for visible-blind UV sensors. Tauc plots ((αhν)2 versus hν for direct bandgap semiconductors) of the absorption spectra are also shown in the inset of Fig. 5. As can be seen, the addition of GQDs has not significantly affected the bandgap of the bare ZnO NR thin film, and only decreases it from 3.3 to 3.27eV. This observation also is appropriate for UV sensing applications.

Photoluminescence intensities of the thin films were studied to find the mechanism governing the effect of GQDs on ZnO NR thin films. Fig. 6 shows the room-temperature PL spectra of the bare ZnO NR thin film and 4GQD-ZnO NR thin film, excited by the wavelength of 325nm. The PL spectrum of the bare ZnO NR thin film shows two bands: (1) a strong ultraviolet band-to-band or near band-to-band emission peaked at 386nm that can be ascribed to the recombination of free excitons, as the characteristic emission of ZnO, and (2) a relatively weaker blue band peaked at 462nm that can be attributed to various structural defects such as impurities and vacancies. The positions of the ultraviolet peak slightly redshifts in the PL spectrum of the 4GQD-ZnO NR thin film, which is in line with the UV–vis absorption spectra. As it is seen, the photoluminescence intensity of 4GQD-ZnO NR thin films is lower than that of the bare ZnO NR thin film. The observation may be because the GQDs can trap the photogenerated electron-hole pairs of ZnO NRs and prevent them from recombination, which leads to a lower photoemission. Although the intensities of both peaks decrease after incorporating GQDs, the decrease is more significant in the UV peak than in the blue peak; the peak1/peak2 ratio decreases from 1.88 for the bare ZnO NR thin film to 1.1 for the 4GQD-ZnO NR thin film. As we said above, the blue peak is mainly due to the structural defects, and the addition of GQDs could increase the defects. Therefore, the decrease in the UV peak is more considerable than in the blue peak.

As it was explained, the prepared GQD-ZnO NR thin films have some advantages over the bare ZnO NR thin film for UV sensing applications:
1)
They have a higher UV absorption, suggesting their higher UV photoresponsivity.
2)
Despite their higher absorption in UV region, there occurs no significant change in their absorption in the visible region of light as compared to the bare ZnO thin film, suggesting that the GQD-ZnO thin films are visible blind nearly as much as the bare ZnO thin film is.
3)
There occurs no significant change in their bandgap energy, which again suggests that the visible-blindness potentials of both thin films are nearly equal.
4)
They have a lower e-h recombination rate, suggesting that more electrons, for a longer time, would be available to contribute to the circuit current when being excited by the UV light. The fact may itself result in a higher UV photocurrent, which may lead to a better UV sensitivity.

Regarding the potential advantages mentioned above, we compared UV sensibility of the prepared thin films. Fig. 7 shows the UV photoresponsivity (passing current) versus time of the nGQDs-ZnO thin films (n =0 to 4) under the UV (365nm) irradiation with a duration of 100s. As it is seen, the 3GQD-ZnO sample has the maximum photogenerated current, with a ~2.75 times higher photocurrent than that of the bare thin film. Further dip-coating procedures result in a lower photocurrent, which could be attributed to the fact that GQDs absorb UV light and if so much GQDs are deposited on the ZnO NRs that can form a layer, they do not allow UV light to reach the ZnO NRs. Moreover, the addition of GQDs has an insignificant influence on the dark current of the bare ZnO thin film. Yang et al. [32] showed that GQDs have a little influence on the dark conductivity of zinc oxide nanorod arrays. None of the samples showed any change in their signals under visible light. Moreover, all samples had a very good signal reproducibility (not shown here).

In order to compare the sensing speeds of the prepared thin films, we fitted on the I-t curves of the bare ZnO and 3GQD-ZnO NR thin films a double-process exponential rise and decay equation [I(t)−I0 =A1 exp(−t/τ1)±A2 exp(−t/τ2)], where I(t) is the passing current at time t, I0 is the asymptotic current under UV illumination, A1 and A2 are some constants, and τ1 and τ2 are fast and slow rise (or decay) time constants, respectively. Fast and slow processes can be attributed to photoresponses originating from bulk and surface interactions with light [44]. Fig. 8 shows the signal rise and decay curves of the bare ZnO and 3GQD-ZnO thin films and their corresponding fitted curves with the corresponding fast and slow time constant values. According to Fig. 8, there occurs a 15% faster bulk-related signal rising and a 33% faster surface-related signal rising (the percentages follow a nearly similar trend regarding the signal decaying). Therefore, it was shown that GQDs could enhance both the photoresponsivity and the sensing speed of ZnO NR thin films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of UV sensing in ZnO
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that when every semiconductor is excited by photons of energies greater than its bandgap, electrons can overcome the forbidden gap and hence they are separated from their hole counterparts and remain for some time in the conduction band. By applying an external bias voltage, the generated electrons can flow around the external circuit. Variations in the circuit current can be used for sensing purposes. In the case of ZnO, because of its bandgap that is around 3.37eV, the exciting photons must be in the UV region of light. The fact is a motivation to use ZnO as UV sensors. The mechanism governing the UV photoresponsivity of ZnO is however not that simple. According to some studies [44,45], the overall mechanism occurs in two major processes: a bulk process and a surface process. The bulk process is the intrinsic generation of excitons from the bulk of ZnO upon excitation by UV light, and it is fast and repeatable. The surface process is due to the surface reaction with ambient, and it is slow. When ZnO, which is natively an n-type semiconductor because of its intrinsic defects like oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitials [46], is exposed to the ambient air, oxygen molecules (O2) and water molecules (H2O) are adsorbed on its surfaces, and ZnO donates its electrons to those molecules, leading to the creation of a low conductivity depletion layer near the surface, as the two following absorption reactions demonstrate:
•
O2 (of ambient air)+e− (of n-type ZnO)→O2 − (on ZnO surface), or
•
2H2O (of ambient air)+O2 (of ambient air)+4e− →4OH−

Now, if ZnO were excited by UV photons, leading to the generation of electron-hole pairs, the holes (with its positive charge) migrate to the surface along the potential slope resulting from the band bending and discharge the negatively charged adsorbed O2 − and OH− ions, and consequently, they are photodesorbed from the surface. The desorption reactions are as follows:
•
h+(UV generated)+O2 − (adsorbed on ZnO surface)→O2
•
4h+(UV generated)+4OH− (adsorbed on ZnO surface)→2H2O+O2

As a result of the two adsorption and desorption reactions, some electrons can be completely freed from their hole counterparts, contributing to the circuit current and resulting in an increase in the conductivity [45,47,48]. Oxygen desorption from the surface of ZnO increases the e–h trapping (life) time.

On the other hand, the nanoscale size of ZnO provides a high surface/volume ratio as compared to the macroscale ZnO, resulting in too many hole-trap states at the surface. Thus, holes can be readily trapped at the surface, leaving alone the unpaired electrons, which consequently increase the conductivity under an applied bias. Therefore, the more the surface states (defects, vacancies, etc.), the more the generated photocurrent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of UV sensing enhancement by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Now we should consider the mechanism of the enhanced photoresponsivity and sensing speed by GQDs. The HOMO level (relative to the vacuum level) of GQDs synthesized via pyrolyzing citric acid as in our study has been measured by Shang et al. [49] as −5.35eV. Our UV–vis analysis of the GQDs suspension reveals the bandgap as 3.3eV. Since LUMO=Eg+HOMO, the LUMO level relative to the vacuum level can be calculated as −2.05eV. Moreover, the electron affinity (conduction band (CB) relative to the vacuum level) of ZnO has been found around −4.2eV [50]. Since our ZnO NRs have a band gap around 3.3eV, its valence band lies at about −7.5eV. Now we can visualize the energy band diagram of our GQD-ZnO NR thin films, as shown in Fig. 9 . When the composite is excited by UV irradiation, electrons in both ZnO and GQD are given enough energies to be transferred to their corresponding conduction bands, leaving behind their hole counterparts. Because ZnO is in contact with GQD, the photogenerated electrons in the GQD conduction band can energetically be transferred to the ZnO conduction band, and the holes in ZnO valence band can energetically be transferred to the GQD conduction band. The process helps to the retardation of recombination, confirming the PL spectroscopy results. The overall result is the longer separation of electrons and holes. Yang et al. [32] argued that the enhancement of photocurrent is due to the interfacial charge transfer from GQDs to their zinc oxide nanorod array. Shao et al. [31] used GQDs to enhance UV sensing properties of ZnO nanorods, and they have shown enhanced photoresponsivity spectra and significantly improved transient photoresponses. However, they described their enhanced photoresponsivity and signal rising speed by a band diagram different from our one, because their GQDs had a LUMO level of −4.4eV. In their band diagram, the LUMO of GQD is energetically below the CB of GQDs, and hence the photogenerated electrons in ZnO can be transferred to the CB of GQD.

On the other hand, since GQD has a high electron mobility, it can facilitate the transport of charge carriers, resulting in a faster UV sensing. Overall, GQDs can effectively provide interfaces for separating excitons and channels for transporting carriers, which led to both an enhanced UV photocurrent and a faster UV sensing. Obviously, the addition of GQDs cannot affect the bulk of ZnO NRs. However, it can affect the surface properties of ZnO NRs. For the same reason, the improvement in the UV sensing speed is higher in terms of the surface-related process, as it is seen in Fig. 8.

In summary, we designed a hybrid MSM structure of graphene quantum dots incorporated within nearly aligned ZnO nanorods on a glass substrate for UV sensing applications. The design aimed at providing a large space between ZnO nanorods to accommodate lots of graphene quantum dots. By dip coating for different times, various amounts of GQDs, synthesized by simply pyrolyzing citric acid, were coated on ZnO NR thin films, prepared on glass substrates by a solvothermal method. The incorporation of GQDs yielded an enhanced UV-excited photocurrent and a faster sensing speed, and the maximum ~2.75 times more photocurrent, than that of the bare thin film was achieved for the three-times dip-coated GQD-ZnO NR thin film. Moreover, the 3GQD-ZnO thin film exhibits a 15% shorter bulk-related time constant and a 33% shorter surface-process time constant as compared to those of the bare ZnO NR thin film. The relatively faster surface-process, as compared to the bulk-process could be attributed to the effect of graphene quantum dots, as confirmed by the PL spectrometry. Finally, the mechanism of the enhanced UV-excited photocurrent was explained by a band diagram, showing that the transfer of electron charge carriers from ZnO NRs to the surrounding GQDs results in a more photocurrent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of Mn on flame spray pyrolysis-made ZnO nanoparticles for flammable gases detection
Proc. SPIE - Int. Soc. Opt. Eng.
